paper_title: WorldMark: A Unified Benchmark Suite for Interactive Video World Models

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Interactive video generation models such as Genie, YUME, HY-World, and Matrix-Game are advancing rapidly, yet every model is evaluated on its own benchmark with private scenes and trajectories, making fair cross-model comparison impossible. Existing public benchmarks offer useful metrics such as trajectory error, aesthetic scores, and VLM-based judgments, but none supplies the standardized test conditions -- identical scenes, identical action sequences, and a unified control interface -- needed to make those metrics comparable across models with heterogeneous inputs. We introduce WorldMark, the first benchmark that provides such a common playing field for interactive Image-to-Video world models. WorldMark contributes: (1) a unified action-mapping layer that translates a shared WASD-style action vocabulary into each model's native control format, enabling apples-to-apples comparison across six major models on identical scenes and trajectories; (2) a hierarchical test suite of 500 evaluation cases covering first- and third-person viewpoints, photorealistic and stylized scenes, and three difficulty tiers from Easy to Hard spanning 20-60s; and (3) a modular evaluation toolkit for Visual Quality, Control Alignment, and World Consistency, designed so that researchers can reuse our standardized inputs while plugging in their own metrics as the field evolves. We will release all data, evaluation code, and model outputs to facilitate future research. Beyond offline metrics, we launch World Model Arena (warena.ai), an online platform where anyone can pit leading world models against each other in side-by-side battles and watch the live leaderboard.

Existing public benchmarks offer useful metrics such as trajectory error, aesthetic scores, and VLM-based judgments, but none supplies the standardized test conditionsidentical scenes, identical action sequences, and unified control interfaceneeded to make those metrics comparable across models with heterogeneous inputs. We introduce WorldMark, the first benchmark that provides such common playing field for interactive Image-to-Video world models. WorldMark contributes: (1) unified action-mapping layer that translates shared WASD-style action vocabulary into each models native control format, enabling apples-to-apples comparison across six major models on identical scenes and trajectories; (2) hierarchical test suite of 500 evaluation cases covering firstand third-person viewpoints, photorealistic and stylized scenes, and three difficulty tiers from Easy to Hard spanning 2060 s; and (3) modular evaluation toolkit for Visual Quality, Control Alignment, and World Consistency, designed so that researchers can reuse our standardized inputs while plugging in their own metrics as the field evolves. We will release all data, evaluation code, and model outputs to facilitate future research. Beyond offline metrics, we launch World Model Arena (warena.ai), an online platform where anyone can pit leading world models against each other in side-by-side battles and watch the live leaderboard. Project Page: https://alaya-studio.github.io/WorldMark/ World Model Arena: https://warena.ai/ Date: April 24,

sections.title: 1 Introduction
sections.content: A World Model [12] should not merely synthesize plausible video frames [44, 4, 13, 38]; it must function as an interactive environment [11, 32] that responds faithfully to user actions and camera controls while preserving long-term scene memory. Recent months have seen surge of systems pursuing this goal through heterogeneous control interfaces: YUME 1.5 [27, 26] accepts WASD keyboard inputs via natural-language captions, HY-World 1.5 [30] takes structured pose commands, Matrix-Game 2.0 [15] exposes custom action functions, and Genie 3 [9], building on the earlier Genie [3], maps gamepad-style controls. Yet each of these models publishes results on its own private benchmark with bespoke trajectories and scenes, making it nearly impossible to determine which model truly advances world modeling and which merely excels under favorable test conditions. Existing public benchmarks do not fill this gap. VBench [18] and VBench++ [17] comprehensively evaluate text-to-video and image-to-video generation quality, but contain no notion of action control or camera trajectory. WorldScore [6] introduces 3D-aware consistency metrics via SLAM-based reconstruction, yet its drone-flythrough viewpoints and passive camera paths are poor proxy for the first-person, actiondriven interaction that characterizes world models. The concurrent MIND benchmark [41] does target interactive generation, but conditions evaluation on ground-truth camera trajectoriesa modality that 1Corresponding authors: kaipeng.zhang@shanda.com, yongtao.ge@shanda.com 1 Figure 1 Overview of WorldMark. Current interactive world models each define their own control interfaces, scenes, and evaluation protocols, making cross-model comparison impossible. WorldMark resolves this fragmentation through three pillars: (a) curated test suite of 500 cases spanning firstand third-person views with both photorealistic and stylized reference scenes, ensuring broad coverage of real-world evaluation conditions. (b) unified action-mapping layer that translates shared action vocabularyWASD for movement and L/R for yaw rotationinto each models native control format (caption prompts, pose parameters, gamepad signals, action vectors, etc.), so that all models receive semantically identical instructions. (c) Under these standard conditions, feeding the same reference image and same action sequence to different models reveals striking quality divergence that proprietary benchmarks obscure. most keyboard-controlled models (YUME, Open-Oasis, Genie) simply cannot accept. Other recent effortsPhyGenBench [28], WorldModelBench [22], and 4DWorldBench [25]assess physical plausibility or 3D/4D consistency but likewise lack standardized interactive action sequences for cross-model comparison. In every case, critical disconnect persists: no benchmark jointly evaluates action controllability, visual quality, and long-horizon world consistency under the interactive paradigm that defines emerging world models. As result, the strikingly divergent outputs produced by current models under the same reference image and action sequence (Fig. 1(c)) remain invisible to existing evaluation protocols. We argue that the root cause of this evaluation gap is not lack of metricstrajectory error [6, 41], aesthetic scores [17], and VLM-based judgments [22, 28, 29] all existbut rather the absence of standardized test conditions. When every model is evaluated on its own scenes, its own trajectories, and its own action definitions, even identical metrics yield incomparable numbers. What the field needs first is common playing field: the same scenes, the same action sequences, and transparent translation layer that feeds each model semantically equivalent inputs through its native control interface. We address this need with WorldMark, the first standardized benchmark purpose-built for interactive Image-to-Video (I2V) world models  (Fig. 1)  . The design centers on two pillars. (i) Unified test infrastructure. We define shared WASD-style action vocabulary and build an actionmapping layer that translates it into each models heterogeneous control formatcaption-based prompts for YUME [26], structured pose parameters for HY-World 1.5 [30], one-hot action tensors for Open-Oasis [5], and so onso that all six evaluated models receive semantically identical instructions on exactly the same scenes. We further curate hierarchical test suite of 500 cases drawn from 50 reference images spanning first-/third-person perspectives and photorealistic/stylized scenes, with three difficulty tiers: Easy (single2 Table 1 Comparison of evaluation benchmarks. Key differentiator: WorldMark provides standardized scenes and action suites enabling unified cross-model comparison for interactive world models. Provides trajectories but no action-to-control mapping for interactive models. Requires GT camera trajectories that most keyboard-controlled models cannot accept. In the table, T2V, I2V, V2V, I2-3D, and Traj cond. denote for Text to Video, Image to Video, Video to Video, Image to 3D, and Trajectory conditioned, respectively.

sections.content: Std. Scenes & Actions Unified Cross-Model

sections.title:
Difficulty
Hierarchy
sections.content: VBench [18] VBench++ [17] WorldScore [6] MIND [41] T2V I2V Traj cond. I2V/I2-3D Traj cond. I2V/V2V WorldMark (Ours) Interactive I2V b segment, 20 s), Medium (two-segment, 40 s), and Hard (three-segment, 60 s) action sequences ranging from simple forward motion to complex patrol routes and 360 rotations. VLM-based scene-aware filtering step automatically selects physically plausible actions for each image, ensuring that the test suite is both challenging and semantically grounded. (ii) Default evaluation toolkit. On top of this standardized infrastructure, we provide ready-to-use metric suite covering three dimensionsVideo Quality, Control Alignment, and World Consistencycombining geometric trajectory metrics with VLM-based scoring. Because all models are evaluated on identical inputs, even simple metrics suffice to reveal meaningful cross-model differences. Meanwhile, the open design allows researchers to plug in their own metrics or scoring models as the field matures. Our main contributions are as follows: We present WorldMark, the first standardized benchmark for interactive I2V world models. Its core is unified action mapper and curated test suite of 500 cases that enable apples-to-apples comparison across six models with heterogeneous control interfaces (YUME 1.5 [26], Matrix-Game 2.0 [15], HY-World 1.5 [30], HY-GameCraft [23, 31], Open-Oasis [5], and Genie 3 [9]). We provide default evaluation toolkit covering Visual Quality, Control Alignment, and World Consistency via both geometric and VLM-based metrics. The toolkit is modular: researchers can reuse our standardized inputs while substituting or extending metrics as needed. We conduct the first systematic cross-model evaluation under controlled conditions, revealing that visual quality and world consistency are largely uncorrelatedthe model producing the most aesthetically pleasing frames (YUME 1.5 [26]) ranks poorly on long-horizon coherence, while the most geometrically consistent model (Genie 3 [9]) has only moderate visual fidelityand that the thirdperson setting remains pronounced failure mode in which several open-source models lose much of their control precision, providing actionable insights and open data for the community.

sections.title: 2.1 Interactive Video Models for World Simulation
sections.content: Video generation has advanced rapidly from early GANand VAE-based methods to diffusion architectures [16, 2] with cameraand motion-controllable extensions [14, 37], and, more recently, to large-scale Video Diffusion Transformers such as Sora [24], CogVideoX [40], HunyuanVideo [20], and Wan [36]. These breakthroughs in quality and duration have prompted researchers to explore video generation models as interactive world simulators [39]. MineWorld [11] jointly models discrete visual and action tokens via an autoregressive Transformer, while Open-Oasis [5] combines DiT backbone with Diffusion 3 Table 2 Evaluation dimensions of WorldMark. Category Metrics Aesthetic Quality; Imaging Quality Visual Quality Control Alignment Translation Error; Rotation Error World Consistency Reprojection Error; State; Content; Style Consistency Forcing for frame-by-frame autoregressive generation; both pioneered action-aware real-time interaction within the Minecraft domain, alongside GameNGen [35] and DIAMOND [1]. Matrix-Game 2.0 [15] and HY-World 1.5 [30] subsequently extended interactive generation to photorealistic open worlds through streaming inference and long-horizon geometric consistency, while closed-loop refinement further improves temporal coherence. HY-GameCraft [23, 31] achieves fine-grained control through unified camera representation, YUME 1.5 [26] enables event-driven exploration via text-controlled generation, and Genie 3 [9] maps gamepad-style controls to open-ended 3D worlds. Despite this rapid progress, the evaluation systems for these models remain fragmented: each typically reports results on private benchmark with its own scenes, trajectories, and metricse.g., yume-Bench for YUME [26], GameWorld Score for Matrix-Game [43], and InterBench for HY-GameCraft-2 [31]. This makes cross-model comparison effectively impossible.

sections.title: 2.2 Evaluation Benchmarks for Video World Models
sections.content: Evaluation standards for video generation have evolved along multiple axes. VBench [18] and VBench++ [17] define multi-dimensional quality metrics for text-to-video and image-to-video generation, respectively. PhyGenBench [28] tests physical common sense across 27 laws, and WorldModelBench [22] probes worldmodel rigor by detecting subtle physical violations such as mass conservation. WorldScore [6] and 4DWorldBench [25] evaluate scene controllability and consistency through explicit camera trajectories and 3D/4D reconstruction quality. The concurrent MIND benchmark [41] directly targets interactive world models, assessing memory consistency and action control at 1080p. Taken together, these benchmarks provide rich set of metricstrajectory error, perceptual distance [42], video fidelity [34], aesthetic scores, VLM-based judgments, physical-violation detectionbut none provides standardized test conditions for interactive models. Each interactive model still evaluates on its own private scenes and action definitions, so even shared metrics produce incomparable numbers. As shown in Tab. 1, WorldMark addresses this gap by supplying common set of scenes, action sequences, and unified control-interface translation layer, so that existing and future metrics can be applied on equal footing across models.

sections.title: 3 WorldMark Suite
sections.content: WorldMark is standardized benchmarking toolkit for interactive image-to-video world models. It comprises five components: (i) an Evaluation Dimension Suite of eight metrics spanning Visual Quality, Control Alignment, and World Consistency; (ii) an Image Suite covering diverse scenes (nature, city, indoor) and viewpoints (first-/third-person); (iii) an Action Suite of 15 trajectories with varying complexity; (iv) Unified Action Interface that translates shared WASD+L/R action vocabulary into each models native control format; and (v) modular Evaluation Workflow tying the above components into four-stage pipeline. Users may also evaluate with custom metrics on our standardized imageaction pairs, ensuring fair cross-model comparison. We introduce each component in turn below.

sections.title: 3.1 Evaluation Dimension Suite
sections.content: As summarized in table 2, WorldMark evaluates generated videos along three complementary dimensions: Visual Quality assesses per-frame fidelity, Control Alignment measures faithfulness to input actions, and World Consistency captures temporal coherence and 3D plausibility. key design principle is multi-level 4 coverage: Visual Quality captures frame-level distortions detectable by image quality models; Control Alignment reconstructs 3D camera poses to verify geometric fidelity against ground-truth trajectories; and World Consistency combines geometric reprojection with VLM-based semantic judgment to detect temporal degradation invisible to either method alone. This layered design ensures that model cannot achieve high aggregate score without excelling across fundamentally different aspects of generation quality. Visual Quality. We evaluate per-frame visual fidelity from two perspectives. Aesthetic Quality. We measure human-perceived aesthetic appeal using the LAION aesthetic predictor [21], which is trained on large-scale human preference data and captures layout, color harmony, lighting balance, and photo-realism. Imaging Quality. Complementing subjective aesthetics, we quantify low-level distortions with MUSIQ [19] trained on the SPAQ [7] dataset, which detects artifacts such as over-exposure, noise, compression, and blur. Control Alignment. Beyond visual quality, it is crucial to verify whether the model faithfully obeys input control signals. We measure this through two pose-based metrics. Translation Error. We evaluate spatial consistency between the generated video and the conditioning camera trajectory. DROID-SLAM [33] reconstructs camera poses from the synthesized frames, and we compute the scale-invariant Euclidean distance: et = tgt st2 (1) where tgt, R3 are the ground-truth and estimated camera positions, and is the least-squares scale factor. This metric ensures the synthesized scene reflects true camera displacements rather than mere 2D pixel shifts. Rotation Error. Complementary to translation, we measure orientational alignment via the geodesic angular deviation between predicted and ground-truth rotation matrices: er = arccos (cid:32) tr(RgtRT) 1 2 (cid:33) 180 π (2) where Rgt, SO(3) are the ground-truth and estimated rotation matrices. Since most evaluated models primarily exhibit yaw-axis motion with negligible roll and pitch, this metric effectively captures heading deviations. World Consistency. This dimension evaluates the models ability to maintain coherent world over time. We employ two complementary approaches: geometric reconstruction and visionlanguage model (VLM) judgment [45]. Reprojection Error. We assess 3D spatial coherence by running DROID-SLAM [33] with its Dense Bundle Adjustment (DBA) layer, which jointly refines camera poses and per-pixel depth. The reprojection error is: ereproj = 1 (i,j)V (cid:13) (cid:13)p (cid:13) ij Π(Pij) (cid:13) (cid:13) (cid:13)2 (3) where is the set of co-visible pixel pairs, ij is the observed 2D coordinate, Pij is the reconstructed 3D point, and Π() denotes camera projection. higher ereproj indicates severe geometric inconsistency, implying that the scenes 3D structure collapses, morphs, or drifts unnaturally over time. Conversely, lower score reflects superior spatial coherence and highly stable 3D geometry throughout the generated video. State Consistency. We evaluate the spatiotemporal stability of objectswhether their shape, texture, and motion remain continuous. VLM is prompted with uniformly sampled frames to track primary subjects and detect abrupt mutations or jerky movements, outputting score reflecting physical consistency. Figure 2 Overview of the Image Suite. Diverse scenes and styles are covered, each shown in first-person and generated third-person views. Content Consistency. This metric penalizes sudden appearance or disappearance of objects, targeting common artifacts such as object hallucination and the popping effect. The VLM compares scene elements across consecutive keyframes and scores the frequency and severity of spatiotemporal hallucinations. Style Consistency. This metric assesses the global visual fidelity and aesthetic uniformity of the generated video, targeting anomalies such as color shifting, unnatural distortions, and fluctuations in the overall artistic or photorealistic style. Given sequence of uniformly sampled frames, the VLM analyzes global visual characteristicsaesthetic tone, lighting, and color paletteand compares them across the timeline to detect stylistic degradation, ultimately producing quantitative score of aesthetic stability.

sections.title: 3.2 Image Suite
sections.content: We curate compact yet diverse set of 50 reference images from the WorldScore dataset, which aggregates images from multiple existing sources. Starting from their curated pool, we remove duplicates and nearduplicate scenes to maximize visual diversity while keeping the total count practical, given the high inference cost of current interactive models. Since the source dataset does not distinguish camera perspectives, we treat all original images as first-person views. To enable cross-viewpoint evaluation, we use an image generation model [10] to synthesize corresponding third-person view for each scene (figure 2), yielding 100 test images in total. The resulting Image Suite spans three scene categories (Nature, City, Indoor), two visual styles (Real, Stylized), and two viewpoints (first-/third-person). The stylized subset covers diverse aesthetics including oil painting, Ukiyo-e, cyberpunk, and Minecraft, ensuring that evaluation is not biased toward photorealistic domains alone. 6 Figure 3 The 15 standardized action sequences, ranging from elementary translations and rotations to combined and cyclic trajectories.

sections.title: 3.3 Action Suite
sections.content: Interactive evaluation requires standardized input actions alongside reference images. We define 15 action sequences of increasing complexity (figure 3): unidirectional translations (actions 14), unidirectional rotation (action 5), combined translation-rotation trajectories (actions 910), and cyclic or repetitive motions (actions 11, 14). All actions are expressed in shared vocabulary of WASD movement plus L/R yaw rotation, and translated to each models native input format via per-model action-mapping adapters (section 3.4). To systematically probe models at increasing levels of difficulty, we organize evaluation into three tiers: Easysingle-segment trajectories lasting 20 (e.g., pure forward motion), testing basic action compliance; Mediumtwo-segment compositions of 40 (e.g., forward then rotate), requiring smooth transitions between movement types; and Hardthree-segment sequences of 60 involving complex patrol routes or 360 panoramic rotations, demanding sustained world consistency over long horizons. Combined with the 100 test images from the Image Suite, this yields approximately 500 standardized evaluation cases. For each reference image, we employ VLM to select contextually plausible actions from this library. The VLM analyzes the scene to identify physical constraintsfor example, lateral obstacles in an enclosed space make prolonged sideways translation implausible (figure 4)and retains only feasible trajectories.

sections.title: 3.4 Unified Action Interface
sections.content: A central challenge in benchmarking interactive world models is that each model consumes actions in proprietary format  (table 3)  : YUME [26] interprets WASD instructions embedded in natural-language captions, HY-World 1.5 [30] and HY-GameCraft [31] take structured 6-DoF pose parameters, Genie 3 [9] accepts gamepad-style controls, Matrix-Game [15] exposes custom action functions, and Open-Oasis [5] operates on 25-dimensional continuous action vectors. This heterogeneity has been primary obstacle to cross-model comparison: even if two models are given move forward, the actual input representation differs entirely. 7 Figure 4 Context-aware action selection. VLM analyzes the initial image to identify physical constraints and selects plausible action sequences from the predefined library. Table 3 Action-mapping adapters translate the shared WASD+L/R vocabulary into each models native control format. Model Native Format Mapping Strategy Caption prompts YUME 1.5 [26] HY-World 1.5 [30] 6-DoF pose params HY-GameCraft [31] 6-DoF pose params Genie 3 [9] Gamepad controls Matrix-Game [15] Action functions Open-Oasis [5] Directional keywords in text Latent timescale matching Pose Plücker ray embeddings Directional button presses Corresponding action API calls 25-dim action vectors Set movement dimensions WorldMark resolves this with unified action interface. We define shared action vocabulary of six discrete primitivesforward (W), backward (S), strafe-left (A), strafe-right (D), yaw-left (L), and yaw-right (R)each parameterized by duration. All 15 trajectories in the Action Suite are composed from this vocabulary. For each supported model, we implement an action-mapping adapter that translates the canonical representation into the models native format. Because individual models differ in angular velocity and movement speed, the adapters calibrate per-model parameters (step size, yaw rate) so that the intended semantic action is faithfully reproduced. This design ensures that all models receive semantically identical instructions while respecting their architectural constraints. Adding support for new model requires implementing only single adapter.

sections.title: 3.5 Evaluation Workflow
sections.content: The preceding subsections define what to evaluate (Evaluation Dimension Suite), what to show the model (Image Suite), what actions to issue (Action Suite), and how to bridge heterogeneous control interfaces (Unified Action Interface). The Evaluation Workflow specifies how these components compose into complete, reproducible evaluation run. Given target model, the user first performs Image Selectionchoosing images by viewpoint, scene category, or visual style, or supplying custom references for which VLM script automatically infers suitable action sequences. The selected images and actions then pass through Action Mapping, where per-model adapters (section 3.4) translate the shared WASD+L/R vocabulary into the target models native control format. With the (text, image, action) triplet constructed, Video Generation runs the model under standardized conditions. Finally, Metric Evaluation scores outputs using the eight-metric suite in table 2. The modular design allows users to plug in custom or third-party metrics (e.g., VBench [18]) without modifying any other stage. 8 Table 4 Quantitative comparison on the First-Person Real scenario. Best results are in bold and second best are underlined. Metric

sections.title: 4.1 Experiment Setup
sections.content: 49.40 68.11 0.222 1.324 0.688 4.151 7.415 3.181 54.79 69.37 0.191 2.079 0.702 5.913 6.352 5. 46.59 49.31 29.31 28.08 45.58 64.14 0.159 6.019 0.376 4.892 0.498 4. 0.447 4.073 5.814 3.726 1.938 2.585 3.748 1.797 0.441 6.416 6.914 8.158 Evaluated Models. Six representative open-source and proprietary interactive I2V world models are benchmarked on WorldMark. The five open-source models are YUME 1.5 [26], Matrix-Game 2.0 [15], HY-World 1.5 [30], HY-GameCraft [23, 31] (abbreviated HY-Game in tables), and Open-Oasis [5]. As representative closed-source model, we evaluate Googles Genie 3 [9]. For first-person evaluation, all six models are tested. For third-person evaluation, only the three models that natively support third-person viewpointsMatrix-Game 2.0, HY-World 1.5, and Genie 3are included. Test Conditions. The Image Suite comprises 50 reference images, categorized into 25 realistic and 25 stylized domains. For each image, we provide paired first-person and third-person views, along with 5 contextually appropriate action sequences selected by VLM. We utilize Nano-Banana-2 [10] for third-person image generation and Gemini-3.1-Pro [8] as the VLM for both automated action selection and video evaluation. All models receive exactly the same reference images and semantically identical action sequences via our unified action interface (section 3.4). We evaluate on four scenario splits: First-Person Real, First-Person Stylized, Third-Person Real, and Third-Person Stylized.

sections.title: 4.2 Quantitative Evaluation Results
sections.content: First-Person View Evaluation. tables 4 and 5 present the quantitative comparison of the evaluated models in the First-Person Real and First-Person Stylized scenarios, respectively. In terms of Visual Quality, YUME 1.5 and HY-World 1.5 demonstrate superior performance. Specifically, YUME 1.5 achieves the highest scores in both Aesthetic Quality (56.94) and Imaging Quality (74.36) in the Real scenario, while HY-World 1.5 shows competitive results, notably taking the lead in Aesthetic Quality (58.50) within the Stylized scenario. Regarding control alignment, the results indicate divergence in model capabilities. HY-Game exhibits robust translation control, achieving the lowest Translation Error in both Real (0.159) and Stylized (0.116) scenarios, as well as the lowest Rotation Error (0.932) in the Stylized setting. Matrix-Game 2.0 also shows strong rotational alignment in the Real scenario (1.324). For World Consistency, the closed-source Genie 3 significantly outperforms the open-source baselines across almost all metrics. In both Real and Stylized scenarios, it consistently achieves the best or second-best results in Reprojection Error, State Consistency, Content Consistency, and Style Consistency. Notably, in the 9 Table 5 Quantitative comparison on the First-Person Stylized scenario. Best results are in bold and second best are underlined. Metric

sections.title: World Consistency
sections.content: Reprojection Error State Consistency Content Consistency Style Consistency YUME 1.5 Matrix-Game 2.0 HY-World 1.5 HY-Game Oasis Genie 3 Method 57.03 69.15 0.223 2.732 0.638 5.891 5.362 4. 47.74 64.24 0.182 1.561 0.672 2.873 6.457 4.934 58.50 64.78 44.02 40.91 30.84 28. 46.84 53.27 0.244 4.316 0.638 6.408 7.159 6.817 0.116 0.932 0.350 3. 0.261 2.835 0.640 4.782 5.196 4.051 1.877 3.523 3.114 2.435 0.256 6.835 7. 7.523 stylized scenario  (table 5)  , Genie 3 dominates all four consistency metrics, highlighting its robust capability in maintaining spatial-temporal coherence and stylistic stability over long-horizon generations. Third-Person View Evaluation. table 6 details the evaluation results for the Third-Person scenarios. Because YUME 1.5, HY-Game, and Open-Oasis lack support for third-person perspective generation, we restrict our comparison to Matrix-Game 2.0, HY-World 1.5, and Genie 3. In these scenarios, HY-World 1.5 establishes clear advantage in Visual Quality, outperforming the others in both Aesthetic and Imaging Quality across both Real and Stylized domains. HY-World 1.5 also demonstrates strong Control Alignment, particularly in the Real scenario, where it achieves the lowest Translation Error (0.206) and Rotation Error (2.137). Consistent with the first-person evaluations, Genie 3 maintains its dominance in World Consistency within the third-person perspective. It achieves the highest scores in State, Content, and Style Consistency in both Real and Stylized settings. While HY-World 1.5 shows competitive Reprojection Error in the Stylized scenario (0.713), Genie 3s overall performance underscores the current advantage of advanced proprietary models in preserving complex world dynamics and temporal consistency during interactive video generation. Real vs. Stylized. Comparing across visual styles, we observe that stylized scenes slightly improve aesthetic scores for some models (HY-World 1.5: 54.7958.50) but degrade control alignmentHY-World 1.5s Rotation Error increases from 2.08 to 4.32. This suggests that non-photorealistic textures introduce additional difficulty or that models struggle with out-of-distribution visual styles. Key Takeaways. Four findings stand out. First, visual quality and world consistency are largely uncorrelated: YUME produces the most appealing frames yet its worlds lack logical coherence, while Genie 3 maintains the most consistent worlds with only moderate frame-level fidelity. Second, strong control alignment does not imply overall qualityHY-Game follows commands precisely but at the cost of visual fidelity, whereas Genie 3 has higher trajectory error yet preserves globally coherent world. Third, third-person generation exposes severe weaknesses: switching from firstto third-person inflates Matrix-Game 2.0s rotation error by roughly an order of magnitude (20), underscoring the difficulty of maintaining camera control around visible character. Fourth, domain-specific training does not transfer: Open-Oasis, trained on Minecraft, fails across all metrics on real-world and stylized scenes.

sections.title: 4.3 Qualitative Evaluation Results
sections.content: figure 5 illustrates qualitative comparison demonstrating high and low performance across Visual Quality, Control Alignment, and World Consistency. In the leftmost example, the top video exhibits high visual 10 Table 6 Quantitative comparison on the Third-Person scenarios. Best results are in bold and second best are underlined. Metric Visual Quality Aesthetic Quality Imaging Quality Control Alignment Translation Error Rotation Error World Consistency Reprojection Error State Consistency Content Consistency Style Consistency Real Stylized Matrix-Game 2.0 HY-World 1.5 Genie 3 Matrix-Game 2.0 HY-World 1.5 Genie 3 52.78 67.26 0.284 27.606 0.814 5.136 3.405 1.659 57.69 70. 0.206 2.137 0.640 6.628 5.707 4.491 51.04 60.20 0.212 14.905 0. 7.082 7.424 8.247 51.60 65.24 0.230 9.211 0.744 3.625 2.083 2. 60.57 66.45 0.220 5.285 0.713 5.274 5.147 7.236 53.76 63.98 0.129 8. 1.148 7.565 7.109 8.541 Figure 5 Qualitative comparison across three evaluation dimensions. The top row demonstrates successful video generation (), while the bottom row illustrates common failures () in Visual Quality, Control Alignment, and World Consistency. fidelity, whereas the bottom video suffers from significant visual degradation. In the middle example, given the action input "R" (camera rotating right), the top video correctly executes the rotational movement; conversely, the bottom video incorrectly translates to the right without the expected change in camera angle. Finally, in the rightmost third-person example, the top video successfully maintains character, background, and stylistic consistency, while the bottom video not only exhibits stylistic shifts but also hallucinates new characters into the scene. We further validate our metrics through Human Preference Alignment study involving 20 volunteers who manually ranked 50 sets of first-person videos from the six models. By comparing these human rankings with our automated WorldMark Evaluation scores, we observe good consistency. Quantitatively, the Spearmans rank correlation between the two sets of results reaches ρ > 0.9. This strong alignment is visually evident in figure 6 : the automated metrics produce line trajectories and model performance hierarchies across Visual Quality, Control Alignment, and World Consistency that are nearly identical to those derived from human preferences. 11 Figure 6 Spearman correlation between human preference rankings and automated metric scores across three World Consistency dimensions. Each point represents an evaluated model. The high ρ values demonstrate strong positive correlation, indicating our automated metrics closely align with human perceptual judgments.

sections.title: 5 Conclusion
sections.content: We presented WorldMark, the first standardized benchmark for interactive I2V world models, featuring unified action interface across six heterogeneous control formats, 500-case test suite, and multi-level evaluation toolkit. Our evaluation reveals that visual quality and world consistency are largely uncorrelated, that precise control alignment does not imply overall generation quality, and that third-person generation remains an open challenge, with the worst-case rotation error degrading by nearly an order of magnitude relative to the first-person setting. We release all data and code; adding new model to WorldMark requires only single action-mapping adapter.
